---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
#format: revealjs
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
  \usetikzlibrary{overlay-beamer-styles}
---

<!-- the section below is shown only if rendering is beamer from latex. (and format:revealjs is commmented) -->

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
% TITLE SLIDES
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{6.1 - Reliability}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{

\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```

## Reliability

In the context of Classical Test Theory, Reliability can be considered the estimate on how "precise" is our test.
\pause
\vfill
More precisely, Reliability is an estimate on how the test is free of _measurement error_



## Classical Test Theory (a refresher)

$X = T + E$
\vfill

$X$ = Observed Score

$T$ = True Score

$E$ = Measurement Error

## True score

![](Figures/True_score.png){fig-align="center" width="70%"}

## Estimating Reliability adequateness

It is typically easier to estimate if realibility is adequate, because it is easier to set recommended values. 
\vfill

\pause But remember, recommended values may vary!

\vfill

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]


% : peggio - meglio
\node[anchor=west] at (0, 0) {\footnotesize \parbox[2cm]{4cm}{\emph{Worse Reliability values}}};
\node[anchor=east] at (11, 0) {\footnotesize \parbox[2cm]{4cm}{\emph{Best Reliability values}}};
\node[anchor=west] at (0, -1.2) {\textbf{Worse}};
\node[anchor=east] at (10, -1.2) {\textbf{Better}};
\draw[thick] (1.5, -1.2) -- (8.5, -1.2);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## Validity and Reliability
\center
![](Figures/Reliability_and_validity_GA.png){fig-align="center" width="60%"}
```{=latex}
\href{https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Reliability_and_validity.svg}{\underline{CC-BY Nevit Dilmen}}
```

## Types of Reliability

- Split-Half Reliability
- Internal Consistency
- Inter-rater Reliability
- Test-Retest Reliability

## Split-Half Reliability

Is the consistency of the score of two random halves of the test

It is calculated by splitting the test in two and then computing the _correlation_ between the two parts (after Spearman-Brown correction)

\pause
**Recommended value**: $\geq 0.7$ (after Spearman-Brown correction)

<!-- Can you guess the problem with split-half reliability-->

## Problem with Split-half reliability

It may depends by the actual random split.

## Internal Consistency

It is an indication on how much items on the same test tapping on the same construct, provide similar results.
\pause

It is often applied _after Factor analysis_ separately on each factor, or accounting for factor structure.


## Internal Consistency: Kuder-Richardson (KR-20)
Is a  way to calculate internal consistency with  dicothomous items (e.g. 0/1 responses)

\pause
**Recommended value**: $\geq 0.7$


## Internal Consistency: Cronbach's alpha
```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\Large
$\alpha$
\normalsize
\end{center}
```


Is a popular way to calculate internal consistency with non dicothomous items (e.g. Likert Scale)

\pause
Mathematically it is the average of all possible split-half reliabilities (under some assumptions)

\pause
**Recommended value**: $\geq 0.7$

## Internal Consistency: Mc Donald's Omega
```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\Large
$\omega$
\normalsize
\end{center}
```
Is a more recommended way to calculate internal consistency.

\pause
It is tightly related to factor analysis

\pause
**Recommended value**: $\geq 0.7$

## Inter-rater Reliability

Is the consistency provided by the scores assigned by two _or more_ *raters* to the same measurement

\pause it is often calculated with **Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC)** (In particular with ICC(2,1) agreement version, but depends on design)

**Recommended value**: $\geq 0.7$

## Test-Retest Reliability
\centering
_Often considered the most important type of reliability_

See the next video lecture!
